skill.name: cover-letter-generator
skill.description: Generates a journal-ready cover letter from manuscript metadata, highlights, and journal-fit notes. Use when preparing an academic submission package and you need editor-facing language that clearly states novelty, relevance, declarations, and corresponding-author details.
skill.summary: Draft submission-ready academic cover letters for journals using structured templates and editorial best practices.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 19 patterns (backtick execution, weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, hardcoded URLs) across 4 files. All 18 findings related to backtick execution, cryptographic algorithms, and system reconnaissance are false positives: backticks in SKILL.md are markdown inline code formatting, cryptographic flags matched coincidental substrings in descriptive text, and reconnaissance flags matched audit report boilerplate. The one hardcoded URL at SKILL.md:7 is a GitHub source attribution link and poses no risk. This skill is a pure markdown instruction file with zero executable code, no network requests, and no filesystem access. A prior independent audit rated it Production Ready at 87/100.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in Source Attribution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 7 contains a hardcoded GitHub URL linking to the skill's source repository. This is standard open-source attribution practice and does not constitute a security risk. The skill makes no programmatic network requests.

content.value_statement: Writing a professional journal cover letter takes time and knowledge of editorial expectations. This skill generates structured, editor-facing cover letters from your manuscript details and target journal information.
actual_capabilities: Generates structured cover letters with salutation, journal fit, novelty, methods, results, declarations, and closing
Validates required inputs and provides missing-input recovery guidance when information is incomplete
Adapts declarations based on journal type using a matrix for engineering, biomedical, and computational journals
Maintains professional and restrained tone without hype language like groundbreaking or revolutionary
References built-in templates and quality checklists to ensure consistent output structure
limitations: Cannot invent manuscript content, results, or declarations that were not provided by the user
Not suitable for peer-review responses, rebuttal letters, or grant cover pages
Requires a target journal to produce a tailored letter; cannot generate useful generic letters
Output quality depends on the completeness and accuracy of user-supplied information

use_cases.title: First-Time Academic Submitter
use_cases.description: A graduate student or early-career researcher preparing their first manuscript submission needs a properly structured cover letter that meets journal editorial expectations.

use_cases.title: Busy Principal Investigator
use_cases.description: A PI with multiple papers in the pipeline wants to quickly generate consistent, professional cover letters for each submission without drafting each one from scratch.

use_cases.title: Research Administrator
use_cases.description: A departmental administrator helping faculty prepare submission packages needs a reliable template-driven approach for generating editor correspondence across different journals.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Cover Letter
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a cover letter for my manuscript titled 'Deep Learning for Medical Image Analysis' for submission to Nature Biomedical Engineering. My key contributions are: a novel attention mechanism for 3D images, 15 percent improvement over baseline on public datasets, and an open-source implementation. Corresponding author is Dr. Jane Smith, University of Science, jane.smith@science.edu.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic submission with minimum required inputs

prompt_templates.title: Cover Letter with Journal Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a cover letter for my paper 'Transformer-Based Protein Structure Prediction' for Bioinformatics. Contributions: new geometric attention layer, faster inference than existing methods, validated on CAMEO benchmark. Methods: deep learning with structural biology constraints. Key result: 20 percent speed improvement with comparable accuracy. Journal fit: Bioinformatics readers value computational methods that solve biological problems. Author: Dr. Alex Chen, BioComp Institute, a.chen@biocomp.edu.
prompt_templates.scenario: Submission with journal-fit rationale and methods summary

prompt_templates.title: Full Submission Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a complete cover letter for The Lancet Digital Health. Title: 'Federated Learning for Multi-Hospital COVID-19 Prognosis.' Contributions: privacy-preserving model trained across 12 hospitals, outperforms single-site models by 30 percent, includes fairness evaluation across demographic groups. Methods: federated averaging with differential privacy guarantees. Results: AUC 0.89 on external validation, equitable performance across age and ethnicity subgroups. Journal fit: the journal emphasizes clinically validated AI with population-level evidence. Author: Prof. Maria Garcia, Global Health AI Center, m.garcia@ghai.org. Ethics: approved by each site IRB. Informed consent: waived for retrospective data. Data availability: aggregate statistics available upon request. Code: GitHub repository under Apache 2.0. COI: none.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complete biomedical journal submission with all declarations

prompt_templates.title: Computational Journal with Reproducibility Emphasis
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a cover letter for a computational journal submission. Manuscript: 'Scalable Graph Neural Networks for Drug Discovery' for Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling. Novelty: new message-passing scheme that scales to million-node molecular graphs, 25 percent improvement in binding affinity prediction, pretrained models for transfer learning. Methods: graph neural networks with attention-based pooling, trained on ChEMBL and PDBbind. Key results: state-of-the-art on 4 of 6 benchmark datasets, linear scaling with molecule size. Include: originality statement, author approval, code availability under MIT license at github.com/example, data availability statement for public datasets used, COI declaration. Suggested reviewers: Dr. First Last (Institution, email) and Dr. First Last (Institution, email). Author: Dr. Robert Kim, Computational Chemistry Lab, r.kim@chem.edu.
prompt_templates.scenario: Computational journal with reproducibility and code availability statements

output_examples.input: Generate a cover letter for 'Deep Learning for Retinal Disease Classification' for JAMA Ophthalmology
output_examples.output: Dear Editor of JAMA Ophthalmology, Please consider our manuscript, 'Deep Learning for Retinal Disease Classification,' for publication in JAMA Ophthalmology. This work aligns with the journal's focus on advances in ophthalmic imaging and AI-driven diagnostics, offering clinically relevant validation across multiple datasets. The manuscript presents a novel ensemble architecture that improves diabetic retinopathy classification accuracy over current benchmarks while maintaining interpretability for clinical review. Our study trained and validated models on retinal images from three hospital systems, achieving high sensitivity and specificity. These findings are directly relevant to ophthalmologists and health-informatics researchers working on deployable screening tools. We confirm that this manuscript is original, has not been published previously, is not under consideration elsewhere, and has been approved by all authors. Ethics approval was obtained from the relevant institutional review board. The authors declare no competing interests. Thank you for your consideration. Sincerely, Dr. [Name], [Affiliation], [Email]

output_examples.input: Cover letter for a computational methods paper on protein folding prediction submitted to Nature Methods
output_examples.output: Dear Editor of Nature Methods, Please consider our manuscript, 'Geometric Deep Learning for Ab Initio Protein Folding Prediction,' for publication in Nature Methods. The manuscript directly addresses the journal's emphasis on novel computational methods that enable biological discovery, providing both methodological innovation and rigorous experimental validation. Our primary contributions are a geometry-aware transformer architecture that reduces protein folding prediction error on novel fold families, and an open-source implementation that runs on commodity GPU hardware. We benchmarked on the CAMEO blind prediction set and CASP targets, achieving state-of-the-art performance on hard targets. We believe this work will interest computational biologists, structural bioinformaticians, and the broader methods-development community. We confirm this manuscript is original, unpublished, not under review elsewhere, and approved by all authors. Code and trained models are available at the provided repository under an MIT license. The authors declare no competing interests. Thank you for your consideration. Sincerely, [Name], [Affiliation], [Email]

output_examples.input: Cover letter with missing information - manuscript for PLoS ONE but no journal fit rationale provided
output_examples.output: Cannot finalize the cover letter yet. Missing required items: journal fit rationale (why this manuscript suits PLoS ONE specifically). Usable fallback: I can draft a partial letter shell after this item is supplied. For PLoS ONE, consider describing how your work meets the journal's criteria for sound methodology and reproducibility regardless of perceived impact.

best_practices: Provide complete manuscript details including title, journal name, and 2-4 core contributions for best results
Review the generated cover letter for accuracy and verify all claims match your actual manuscript before submission
Use the journal-specific declaration matrix to ensure required ethical and availability statements are included for your field
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to generate letters without a target journal; generic cover letters reduce acceptance chances
Do not rely on the skill to invent missing results, methods details, or declarations that you have not provided
Do not submit the generated letter without verifying that novelty claims and contribution statements match your manuscript

faq.question: What inputs do I need to provide to generate a cover letter?
faq.answer: You need a manuscript title, target journal, 2-4 core contributions, and corresponding author details. Additional recommended inputs include journal fit rationale, methods summary, key results, and optional declarations.

faq.question: Can this skill write a cover letter without a target journal?
faq.answer: No. The skill requires a target journal to tailor the journal-fit paragraph and declarations. Using it without one produces a generic letter that journals do not value.

faq.question: What happens if I am missing some required information?
faq.answer: The skill will tell you which items are missing and offer to draft a partial letter shell after you supply the missing items. It will not invent information you do not provide.

faq.question: Can this skill handle rebuttal letters or reviewer responses?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed only for initial manuscript submission cover letters. It does not support peer-review responses, revision letters, or grant cover pages.

faq.question: How does the skill handle different types of journals?
faq.answer: It uses a journal-specific declaration matrix to include appropriate statements for engineering journals, biomedical or clinical journals, and computational journals.

faq.question: Does the skill invent content if I do not provide enough details?
faq.answer: No. The skill will ask for missing information rather than inventing results, methods, or declarations that were not supplied. It is designed for editorial language, not content creation.
